jobs.description: 🟢 Seamless Gutter Services - Gutter Repair - Service Call
Roof Cleaning - One Storey (up to 2000 sq. ft.)

notes.content:
Step‑by‑Step Procedure for the Technician

Pre‑Site Coordination and Inspection
1.1. Notify the store manager and schedule work when customer traffic is low.
1.2. Inspect the replacement tile for correct size, color, pattern and any defects
krausflooring.com
; remove it from packaging and let it acclimate in the store for at least 24 hours
urbansurfaces.com
.
1.3. Review safety data and consult a professional if the building or adhesive predates 1980; adhesives manufactured before 1980 may contain asbestos
hunker.com
.

Site Preparation
2.1. Clear merchandise and fixtures from the work area. Mark the area with caution tape and lay down protective drop cloths.
2.2. Gather tools and PPE (see lists below). Put on safety glasses, respirator, gloves and knee pads
urbansurfaces.com
.
2.3. Vacuum the area to remove loose debris and dust. Ensure good ventilation.

Removal of the Damaged Carpet Tile
3.1. Loosen the tile: If the tile is loose lay, lift a corner using a putty knife or utility knife. For glued tiles, gently insert the putty knife under a corner and pry up. Warm the tile with a hair dryer or heat gun to soften the adhesive
moderncarpetcleaningburswood.com.au
; work slowly to avoid tearing the adjacent tiles.
3.2. Pry up the tile: Slide the putty knife or small pry bar under the tile, working around its edges. Avoid brute force to prevent subfloor damage. Continue prying until the tile lifts free; remove it carefully and set aside.

Adhesive and Residue Removal
4.1. Mechanical scraping: Use a floor scraper or putty knife to scrape off residual adhesive from the concrete or wood subfloor. Work in sections, applying firm, even pressure
thisoldhouse.com
.
4.2. Avoid harsh solvents: Do not use solvent‑based or citrus adhesive removers because residue can affect new adhesive
urbansurfaces.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
. For asphalt (cut‑back) adhesives, wet scrape the adhesive to a thin, well‑bonded residue and encapsulate it using a manufacturer‑approved product such as BC9000
qmsview.shawinc.com
. For stubborn glue, mix ½ cup trisodium phosphate (TSP) with 2 gallons of hot water, scrub the adhesive, and rinse thoroughly; wear gloves, goggles and a respirator and ventilate the area
hunker.com
.
4.3. Clean and inspect: Vacuum or sweep the subfloor; remove dust and debris. Ensure the subfloor is smooth, clean, dry and free of contaminants
urbansurfaces.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
. Fill any cracks or depressions with a non‑shrinking cement‑based patching compound
urbansurfaces.com
 and allow to dry. If adhesive residue cannot be completely removed, apply a minimum 1/8‑inch cement‑based underlayment to isolate it
urbansurfaces.com
.

Dry‑Fit and Cutting the New Tile
5.1. Position the replacement tile in the opening without adhesive to check fit and orientation. Align the arrow on the tile’s back with the direction of adjacent tiles to maintain pile direction
hunker.com
.
5.2. If trimming is needed, mark the cut lines on the back of the tile and cut with a sharp utility knife using a straightedge
urbansurfaces.com
. Cut from the backside to avoid fraying.

Applying Adhesive
6.1. Select a pressure‑sensitive adhesive compatible with the carpet tile backing (e.g., Kraus KPA‑51/KPA‑99 or Shaw 4151/4252) and check moisture/pH limits if installing over concrete
krausflooring.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
.
6.2. Apply adhesive only to the prepared subfloor using a 3/8‑inch nap paint roller or a U‑notch trowel sized per manufacturer specifications (e.g., 1/32 in. × 1/16 in. × 1/32 in.)
krausflooring.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
. Spread adhesive evenly; do not apply over cables or flat‑wire systems
krausflooring.com
.
6.3. Allow the adhesive to dry until tacky as instructed. Installing into wet adhesive can create a permanent bond and make future replacement difficult
qmsview.shawinc.com
.
6.4. For self‑adhesive tiles or glue‑free systems, remove the protective liner to expose the adhesive or apply adhesive dots to the tile corners
hunker.com
.

Installing the New Carpet Tile
7.1. Place one corner of the tile into the opening and “tip” it into place, ensuring alignment with adjacent tiles
krausflooring.com
. Slide the tile gently to avoid trapping fibers
qmsview.shawinc.com
.
7.2. Press down firmly across the entire tile surface. Roll the tile with a hand roller or a 75–100 lb floor roller to ensure complete adhesion and eliminate air pockets
urbansurfaces.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
.
7.3. Check that the seams are tight and level. Use a seam roller to blend seams and trim any loose fibers
urbansurfaces.com
.

Finishing and Cleanup
8.1. Wipe away any excess adhesive with a damp cloth before it dries. Vacuum or sweep the area to remove loose fibers or debris
moderncarpetcleaningburswood.com.au
.
8.2. Keep heavy foot traffic off the new tile for at least 24 hours to allow the adhesive to cure
moderncarpetcleaningburswood.com.au
.
8.3. Dispose of the removed tile and debris according to local regulations. If adhesive contains hazardous materials such as asbestos, follow regulated disposal procedures and consult a licensed abatement contractor.
8.4. Remove protective coverings, return furniture to its original position, and conduct a final inspection with the store manager to confirm satisfaction.

Tool & Equipment List
Tool/Equipment	Purpose	Notes
Personal protective equipment (PPE)	Safety glasses, respirator, gloves and knee pads protect against adhesive fumes and sharp tools
urbansurfaces.com
hunker.com
	Ensure respiratory protection if using TSP or working with potential asbestos.
Utility knife with extra blades	Cutting seams, scoring and trimming carpet tiles
moderncarpetcleaningburswood.com.au
urbansurfaces.com
	Cut from the tile’s backside for clean edges.
Putty knife or small pry bar	Lifting corners of glued tiles and scraping adhesive
moderncarpetcleaningburswood.com.au
thisoldhouse.com
	Use a wide blade to avoid gouging the subfloor.
Hair dryer or heat gun	Softens pressure‑sensitive adhesive under the tile for easier removal
moderncarpetcleaningburswood.com.au
	Use low heat; avoid overheating to prevent damage.
Floor scraper	Removing stubborn glue from concrete or wood subfloors
thisoldhouse.com
	Keep blade sharp for efficient scraping.
Scrub brush and TSP cleaning solution	Removing stubborn adhesive residues (1/2 cup TSP per 2 gallons hot water)
hunker.com
	Use only when mechanical scraping fails; wear full PPE and ventilate area.
Vacuum or broom & dustpan	Cleaning dust and debris from the subfloor before and after work
urbansurfaces.com
	A shop vacuum helps remove fine dust.
Chalk line, tape measure and straightedge/square	Marking reference lines and cutting tiles accurately
urbansurfaces.com
	Ensure replacement tile fits precisely.
Trowel or 3/8‑inch nap paint roller	Applying pressure‑sensitive adhesive evenly
krausflooring.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
	Choose trowel notch size per adhesive manufacturer.
Hand roller or 75–100 lb floor roller	Pressing tile into adhesive to ensure full bond
urbansurfaces.com
qmsview.shawinc.com
	Use a seam roller for blending seams.
Mineral spirits (optional)	Cleaning tools or minor adhesive residue
urbansurfaces.com
	Use sparingly; not for removing cutback adhesives.
Drop cloths and caution tape	Protecting adjacent flooring and marking work area	Maintain a safe environment for store staff and customers.

Thanks much… here's some general info about our situation.  I can send pictures if you'd like.

Our address is 14900 Beau Vue Dr., Little Rock 72223

Michael Carenbauer
501–766–2441



I am reaching out to request a quote for gutter cleaning and maintenance on our home in Little Rock. The house is approximately 5,000 sq. ft., with gutters located on both the front and rear.

The gutters are routed underground, and we have noticed some issues with drainage that may indicate clogs or other obstructions in the system. Additionally, a fox recently caused some damage to part of the guttering, which may need to be inspected and repaired.

Could you please provide an estimate for cleaning and maintenance, and let us know if you handle underground line flushing and minor repairs of this nature?

Thank you for your time and assistance. I look forward to hearing from you.
